claim: AI is already inside your organization.
footnote: In this benchmark, ninety-two percent of nonprofits report using AI in some capacity, while much of that use is described as individual and reactive rather than organization-wide and governed. That combination is what researchers mean when they say tools have arrived before the rulebook, exactly the situation your sentence names.

claim: Human foundation precedes technological implementation (workflow redesign correlate).

cite.author: McKinsey & Company
cite.title: The State of AI in 2025: Agents, Innovation, and Transformation

footnote: McKinsey’s analysis ties meaningful financial impact from AI to organizations that have redesigned workflows, not merely deployed models. Readers can fairly hear your ordering of stages as tracking that finding: structural and human readiness comes before scaling technical solutions.

claim: Most organizations jump straight to tech or freeze; ~5–7% high-performer cohort.

cite.author: McKinsey & Company; Boston Consulting Group; MIT NANDA Initiative
cite.title: Convergent surveys on AI adoption versus realized value (2025)
cite.detail: McKinsey n = 1,993 · BCG n = 1,250 firms · MIT NANDA interviews and deployment review.

footnote: Independent studies in the corporate and nonprofit literature repeatedly find broad experimentation paired with a narrow band reporting strong outcomes, often summarized as roughly five to seven percent of organizations in a “high performer” posture. Your language compresses that pattern; footnotes on the live site can name each study when you quote a number.

claim: Safety first, governance that answers the questions your people are asking.

cite.author: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
cite.title: AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
cite.detail: Voluntary U.S. consensus framework for managing AI risks.

footnote: NIST’s framework is written so that boards and operating leaders can ask repeatable questions about risk, trustworthiness, and accountability before systems spread. It does not dictate theology or mission, but it gives civil-society organizations a publicly recognized vocabulary for what “Safety” means in practice.

claim: Sandbox, Training, Tech: staged maturity.

cite.author: Vatican (Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith and Dicastery for Culture and Education)
cite.title: Antiqua et Nova: Note on the Relationship Between AI and Human Intelligence

footnote: The note insists that artificial intelligence should complement human intelligence rather than replace its moral and relational depth, and warns against deskilling and surrendering judgment to machines. That ethical stance aligns with treating experimentation and formation as stages before wholesale automation, without claiming any one vendor methodology.

claim: Credibility is increasingly relational in an AI-saturated world.

cite.author: Pew Research Center
cite.title: Americans’ Views on Identifying AI Use in What They See and Read
cite.detail: Survey of 5,023 U.S. adults (June 9–15, 2025 fieldwork); reported Sept. 2025.

footnote: Pew finds that about 76% of U.S. adults say it is extremely or very important to tell whether pictures, videos, or text were made by AI or by people, while 53% are not too or not at all confident they can spot the difference. That gap between importance and confidence is one rigorous way to describe why credibility today travels through trusted people and institutions, not through polish alone.

claim: We're all beginners; lead anyway, start with safety; build for movement.
footnote: This line is pastoral and rhetorical rather than a statistical claim. On the public site it gains credibility when it sits beside named surveys (above) and named governance frameworks, not when it pretends to be a finding from a lab.

claim: Tailored for nonprofits, churches, institutions.

cite.author: Movemental
cite.title: Research Corpus: AI Adoption, Governance, and Formation (v1.0)
cite.detail: Internal synthesis with verified primary citations.

footnote: The corpus gathers sector-specific adoption and governance evidence for nonprofits and churches (including Virtuous, TechSoup, Lifeway, Barna/Pushpay, and Barna/Gloo), alongside cross-sector studies used for context. It exists so sentences like yours can be unpacked into footnotes readers can actually verify.

claim: Rush ahead vs stand still, two traps; neither yields responsible adoption.
footnote: The nonprofit data describe both unchecked individual adoption and reactive use without shared workflows, patterns leadership teams often experience as “too fast in the shadows” or “frozen while staff move anyway.” Your two-column framing dramatizes tensions those surveys measure rather than invent.

claim: Sequence matters, skipping stages fragments the organization.
footnote: Forvis Mazars argues that AI expands nonprofit boards’ traditional fiduciary duties, care, loyalty, and obedience, and should be folded into enterprise risk management rather than treated as a siloed tech experiment. That legal-and-governance framing supports treating early governance (Safety) as structurally prior to scaled deployment.

claim: You don't need to master AI, you need a path for leading through it.
footnote: This is leadership counsel, not a citation to a single paper. Pair it on the site with McKinsey’s distinction between tool adoption and workflow transformation so readers see both humility about technique and seriousness about organizational change.

claim: Staff use AI for email, summaries, content without org-set boundaries.
footnote: The report documents pervasive use coupled with reactive, individual patterns and the absence of shared workflows for most respondents, precisely the “boundaries set by individual discretion” situation your copy describes.

claim: Sensitive ministry or donor-adjacent data in consumer AI tools creates liability.

cite.author: FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center
cite.title: 2025 IC3 Annual Report (AI-related complaints and losses)

footnote: Federal complaint data document large and rising dollar losses tied to AI-enabled fraud, including categories such as voice cloning, showing that “quiet liability” is not hypothetical at national scale. Pair this with your own counsel on what must never be pasted into unmanaged models.

claim: Undisclosed AI damages authenticity and trust.

cite.author: Barna Group & Pushpay
cite.title: Technology for Missional Impact: State of Church Tech 2026

footnote: Church leaders surveyed report intense concern that AI could erode congregational trust and undermine authenticity in preaching. Those attitudes supply empirical texture for why undisclosed machine-generated pastoral voice is not merely a technical breach but a relational one.

claim: Large language models carry embedded worldviews; uncritical use imports assumptions.
footnote: Gloo’s benchmark documents systematic gaps between leading models and Christian theological and ethical priorities across curated question sets, empirical support for the claim that models are not culturally neutral processors.

claim: Skipping Safety before Tech invites breach and broken trust.

cite.author: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
cite.title: AI Risk Management Framework

footnote: NIST’s framework is explicitly about managing risk across the lifecycle of AI systems, mapping, measuring, and governing before reliance scales. It gives readers an independent anchor for why “order” is not cosmetic.

claim: Guided path: safety, capability, mission-aligned solutions.
footnote: The corpus narrative sections summarize convergent evidence that organizations adopt AI faster than they govern it and faster than they capture durable value, exactly the tension a staged consulting path claims to address.

claim: AI is already being used inside your organization.
footnote: TechSoup’s benchmark finds extremely high rates of AI awareness and exploration among surveyed nonprofits, with only a minority reporting a formal strategy, another angle on the same reality: usage and curiosity precede institution-wide guardrails.

claim: Safety gives leaders shared boundaries without panic.

cite.author: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
cite.title: AI Risk Management Framework Playbook

footnote: The Playbook translates the framework into actionable tasks, exactly the sort of structured calm your sentence promises when leaders fear either hype or paralysis.

claim: Very high nonprofit AI use; low documented workflows; nearly half without governance policy.
footnote: Key figures from the study: ninety-two percent use AI in some capacity; eighty-one percent report individual rather than shared workflows; forty-seven percent report no AI governance policy; four percent report documented repeatable workflows. Together they justify urgency about collective standards.

claim: Pastors and churches are actively using or experimenting with AI; formal policies are rare.

cite.author: Barna Group & Pushpay
cite.title: Technology for Missional Impact: State of Church Tech 2026

footnote: Sixty percent of church leaders personally use AI at least monthly; thirty-three percent say their church uses AI in ministry or operations; only five percent report an established AI policy, numbers that ground “shadow ministry AI” narratives in survey evidence rather than rumor.

claim: Large shares of pastors worry about reliability, disclosure, and whether AI is a fitting substitute for human ministry.
footnote: Four in five pastors worry generated content must be edited for errors; three in five express concern about non-disclosure; fifty-five percent affirm that God has shared his Word through people and “AI isn’t a person.” Those findings lend empirical weight to ethics-first messaging.

claim: Reconstructed case study, illustrative composite, not one named client.
footnote: Standard scholarly practice for composite illustrations is to label them clearly on the page itself so readers do not mistake narrative vividness for a testimonial. No survey substitutes for that honesty.

claim: If staff already use consumer AI, the organization is already exposed.
footnote: High measured prevalence of AI use among nonprofits implies that “no official AI program” does not mean “no AI touchpoints.” Exposure here means operational reality, not moral blame, a distinction worth keeping in the FAQ voice.

claim: This is adaptive leadership and formation, not primarily an IT ticket.
footnote: Smith’s argument that habits and technologies shape desire helps readers see AI adoption as a formation question, what kind of people and communities we become, not only a procurement question.

claim: Tools shape culture; untethered tool use fragments norms.

cite.author: Daniel H. Pink
cite.title: Drive: The Surprising Truth About What Motivates Us

footnote: Pink’s synthesis on autonomy, mastery, and purpose is frequently used in adult learning design to explain why scattershot tool adoption without shared practice fails to build collective competence, supporting your cultural claim at the level of organizational psychology.

claim: Transparent AI use; infrastructure not ghostwritten authorship.

cite.author: Vatican (Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith and Dicastery for Culture and Education)
cite.title: Antiqua et Nova

footnote: The note warns against treating AI as a substitute for human moral agency and encourages discernment about where machine assistance complements rather than supplants human responsibility, parallel to your distinction between infrastructure and authored voice.

claim: Formation-led skills building (cohort learning).

cite.author: David A. Kolb
cite.title: Experiential Learning: Experience as the Source of Learning and Development

footnote: Kolb's cycle (concrete experience, reflective observation, abstract conceptualization, active experimentation) is the standard academic basis for cohort-based adult learning. It explains to readers why Training is framed as formation, not a one-hour webinar.

claim: Staff already use consumer LLMs when leadership has not set boundaries.
footnote: Personal monthly AI use among church leaders is common even while formal church-wide adoption and written policies lag, a church-facing illustration of the broader “shadow IT” pattern.

claim: Mission organizations trade in trust; automation choices affect relationships.

cite.author: Association of Fundraising Professionals
cite.title: Code of Ethical Standards (and related guidance on integrity in outreach)

footnote: Professional fundraising ethics foreground honesty, transparency, and stewardship of donor trust, canonical language for why undisclosed automation in constituent-facing work is not a niche IT concern.

claim: Trusted practitioners anchor judgment about AI and formation.
footnote: Named leaders listed on the Voices page should carry short bibliographic lines (books, institutions, publisher pages) in the UI proper, footnotes here cannot replace primary bio verification each time you quote someone.

claim: Movement leaders are an ecosystem layer, not a fourth sales audience.

cite.author: Movemental
cite.title: Movement Leaders as Ecosystem Layer (strategy memorandum)

footnote: This memorandum defines information architecture and voice rules for how Movemental distinguishes implementation audiences from movement-leader ecology. Publish a reader-facing summary if you want external readers to verify the claim without internal file access.

claim: Published prices for Safety, Sandbox, Training, Tech, Bundle.
footnote: Pricing is contractual fact: the authoritative “source” is the quote or order form the customer signs. On the site, match every dollar figure across pages so readers never see conflicting menus.

claim: Do not publish uncited “68%” detection or “40–60% AI web” lines without primary sources.

cite.author: Movemental
cite.title: AI Credibility Crisis: Internal Evidence Synthesis

footnote: That memo traces how fragile headline numbers entered Movemental copy and points to stronger replacements, such as Pew’s importance-versus-confidence gap for detection, or detector-limited web prevalence studies, so public footnotes stay honest.

claim: AI capability and adoption are moving quickly worldwide.
footnote: The AI Index compiles annual indicators on models, investment, and usage, useful backstop when you want readers to feel the sheer pace of change without leaning on any one vendor infographic.

claim: 91% of church leaders support using AI in ministry. Most use it weekly.

cite.author: Barna Group & Gloo
cite.title: State of the Church 2026: AI research series
cite.detail: AI Reality research synthesis · church leader adoption

footnote: Movemental's AI Reality research synthesis reports broad pastoral support for AI in ministry alongside high weekly use. The Safety page cites this figure to show adoption has outpaced governance.

claim: About 9% have written any policy for it.

cite.author: Barna Group & Pushpay
cite.title: Technology for Missional Impact: State of Church Tech 2026
cite.detail: n = 1,306 U.S. church leaders · AI Reality research synthesis

footnote: The same research stream finds formal AI policies remain rare while personal use is common. The Safety page uses the nine percent figure as the governance gap, not as a substitute for your own board's audit.

claim: 60% are concerned about cloned-voice fraud, and 1 in 4 say AI scams have already reached their community.

cite.author: Barna Group & Gloo
cite.title: State of the Church 2026: AI research series
cite.detail: Voice-cloning concern and community scam exposure · AI Reality research

footnote: These figures ground the impersonation response plan inside the Safety Handbook. They are cited on the Safety page because cloned-voice fraud is an active credibility threat, not a hypothetical efficiency problem.
